---
title: "mpshock"
subtitle: "Cross-country monetary policy shocks in R"
author: "Charles Coverdale"
institute: "London, United Kingdom"
date: "2026-04-01"
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
format:
  beamer:
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 11pt
    navigation: empty
    theme: default
    colortheme: default
    fonttheme: default
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    include-in-header: header.tex
    slide-level: 2
    keep-tex: false
    fig-align: center
    classoption:
      - t
---


## The question

\vspace{1.5em}

\begin{center}
\Large\bfseries\color{econblue}
How do we make identified monetary policy shock series\\[0.1em]
reproducible, comparable, and cross-country in a single R call?
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}

\begin{center}
\normalsize\itshape
Thirteen series, three countries, five identification strategies, one tidy schema.
\end{center}

::: notes
Open with the question, not the package. Identified shock series are
the standard input to IRFs, local projections, and proxy-SVARs. Right
now they live as CSVs on personal web pages with inconsistent formats.
We need one call.
:::


## Why it matters

- **Empirical macroeconomists** run impulse responses under several identification strategies as a standard robustness check
- **Policy institutions** (central banks, Treasuries) want cross-country comparability of monetary stance measures
- **Graduate students and replicators** lose hours assembling shock files before the interesting analysis begins
- **Forecasters and market strategists** feed shock series into nowcasting pipelines and scenario models

::: notes
Four stakeholder groups. Point at the graduate student case: every
applied monetary paper burns time on data plumbing before the
identification discussion even starts. 60 seconds.
:::



## What is already out there

- **Personal replication files**: Nakamura-Steinsson on Harvard Dataverse, Bauer-Swanson on the SF Fed, Jarocinski-Karadi on personal pages
- **`hfdshocks`** (GitHub): single-country, unmaintained, no provenance metadata
- **Bank of England UKMPD spreadsheet**: live-maintained but UK-only, Excel format
- **Individual central-bank bulletins**: Atlanta Fed (Wu-Xia), RBA (Hambur-Haque, Beckers)

\vspace{0.5em}

The gap: **no R package bundles shock series for more than one country in a common schema**.\footnote{\scriptsize Aeberhardt et al.\ (2024) report pairwise correlations of 0.4 to 0.7 across alternative US shock measures on common samples; reporting several identifications in parallel is now standard.}

::: notes
Respect existing archives. They work for their own series but nothing
stitches them together. mpshock is the first multi-country bundle in R.
45 seconds.
:::



## What mpshock offers

1. **Coverage**: 13 series across US (8), UK (3), and Australia (2), spanning narrative, high-frequency, sign-restriction, informationally-robust, and shadow-rate identifications
2. **Interface**: every series loads via `mp_shock(series, start, end)` and returns a tidy `data.frame` with the `mp_shock` S3 class; outputs compose via `rbind()`
3. **Provenance**: each series carries DOI, source URL, licence, and download date; pure data + computation, no runtime network, 240 unit tests

\vspace{0.5em}

On CRAN since April 2026. Plugs directly into `lpirfs`, `BVAR`, and `vars`\footnote{\scriptsize Adammer (2019) \textit{lpirfs}, Kuschnig \& Vashold (2021) \textit{BVAR}, Pfaff (2008) \textit{vars}, all R packages for impulse response estimation.}.

::: notes
Three bullets, three selling points. Emphasise the cross-country
coverage: that is the novel contribution. Downstream compatibility is
the reason empirical users will adopt. 45 seconds.
:::



## US shocks: cumulative paths across four identifications

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig1_cumulative_us.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Cumulative monthly US monetary policy shocks under four identifications over a common sample.}
```

::: notes
First gallery slide. All four identifications broadly track each other
through the ZLB period but diverge at key inflection points. Point at
the 2008-2009 drop. 25 seconds.
:::


## US shock correlations: 0.4 to 0.7 across identifications

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig2_correlations.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Pairwise Pearson correlations among seven US shock and stance series, common monthly sample 2000-02 to 2014-03.}
```

::: notes
The empirical punchline. Correlations in the 0.4 to 0.7 block confirm
alternative identifications capture related but distinct variation.
Report IRFs under several shocks rather than selecting one. 25 seconds.
:::


## Coverage: thirteen series on one timeline

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig3_coverage.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Time coverage of all thirteen bundled series across the US (8), UK (3), and Australia (2); each row marks the native publication window.}
```

::: notes
Second gallery slide. US series cluster around 1995-2015, UK runs
1997-present via BoE UKMPD, AU picks up from 2004. Coverage is the
bottleneck for cross-country work. 25 seconds.
:::


## Distributions: shock magnitudes by series

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig4_distribution.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Distribution of nonzero monthly shock magnitudes by series, in native units. Narrative series show heavier right tails; high-frequency surprises are tighter.}
```

::: notes
Fourth gallery slide. Ties directly to the standardisation step on the
hero chart. Narrative series carry larger swings than HFS surprises;
standardisation is how you make them comparable. 25 seconds.
:::


## Central formulas

\vspace{0.3em}

\textbf{High-frequency surprise} (30-minute window around FOMC):
\begin{equation}
\mathrm{HFS}_t = f_t^{+} - f_t^{-}
\end{equation}

\textbf{Orthogonalised shock} (Bauer-Swanson residual):
\begin{equation}
\mathrm{MPS\_ORTH}_t = \mathrm{MPS}_t - X_t^{\prime}\hat{\beta}
\end{equation}

\textbf{Informationally-robust shock} (Miranda-Agrippino-Ricco):
\begin{equation}
u_t = \mathrm{FF4}_t - \sum_{h=0}^{4} \gamma_h \, \Delta \mathbb{E}_t^{\mathrm{GB}}[y_{t+h}]
\end{equation}

::: notes
Three equations, three identification strategies. Do not read them
aloud. The HFS is the primitive; the Bauer-Swanson residual removes
predictable pre-announcement variation; MAR projects the surprise onto
Greenbook revisions to handle the information effect. 60 seconds.
:::



## Package at a glance

::: columns
::: {.column width="58%"}

Bundled series by country:

- **United States**: 8 series (Nakamura-Steinsson, Bauer-Swanson, GSS target, GSS path, Jarocinski-Karadi MP and CBI, Miranda-Agrippino-Ricco, Wu-Xia)
- **United Kingdom**: 3 series (UKMPD three-factor, Cesa-Bianchi, Cloyne-Hurtgen)
- **Australia**: 2 series (Hambur-Haque three-factor, Beckers)
- **Loader + metadata**: `mp_shock()`, `mp_list()`, `mp_source()`
- **Helpers**: `mp_align()`, `mp_to_quarterly()`, `mp_cumulate()`

Deps: `cli`, `stats`, `utils`. R $\geq$ 4.1.0.

:::
::: {.column width="40%"}

\begin{block}{Uniform output}
Every series returns:

\texttt{date} | \texttt{shock} | \texttt{series}

Multi-factor series add\\ \texttt{path}, \texttt{qe}, or \texttt{term\_premium}.

Outputs compose via \texttt{rbind()} into panel data.
\end{block}

:::
:::

::: notes
Two columns. Left lists the series by country. Right highlights the
output schema in the blue block. The consistency is the pitch. 45
seconds.
:::



## Minimal working example

```r
library(mpshock)

# Three US identifications on a common monthly sample
ns  <- mp_shock("nakamura_steinsson", start = "2000-02-01")
bs  <- mp_shock("bauer_swanson",      start = "2000-02-01")
jk  <- mp_shock("jarocinski_karadi_mp", start = "2000-02-01")

# Compose into a panel for plotting or modelling
panel <- rbind(ns, bs, jk)

# Hand off to downstream IRF estimation
lpirfs::lp_lin_iv(endog_data, shock = panel$shock)
```

\footnotesize
All three calls share the same three-column schema, so `rbind()` works
without reshaping or renaming.

::: notes
Live-read the code. Three calls, three data frames, one rbind, one
handoff to lpirfs. That is the entire user experience before you start
doing identification-robustness IRFs. 60 seconds.
:::



## Cross-country monetary policy shocks, 2005 to 2022

**Data.** `bauer_swanson` (US MPS_ORTH), `ukmpd` Target factor (UK), `hambur_haque_au` action factor (AU). Each standardised by full-sample standard deviation of nonzero shocks before cumulating.

**Question.** *Do identified monetary policy shocks in the US, UK, and Australia move together around major global events, or does country-specific identification dominate?*

**Why this case.** First published cross-country cumulative shock chart from a single R pipeline\footnote{\scriptsize No prior R package bundles shock series for more than one country. See paper section on cross-country shocks for full discussion.}. Covers GFC, 2013 taper tantrum, COVID-19, and 2022-23 tightening cycle. Standardisation makes scales comparable despite different native units.

::: notes
Setup only. No results on this slide. Point forward: hero figure is
next. Name the three series, the standardisation, and why the
comparison is novel. 45 seconds.
:::



## Hero result: US, UK, and AU shocks move together through global shocks

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.68\textheight]{figures/hero_figure.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Cumulative standardised monetary policy shocks for the US, UK, and Australia. Shaded bands: GFC (Sep 2008 to Jun 2009) and COVID-19 (Mar to Jun 2020). Sources: Bauer-Swanson (US), UKMPD Target (UK), Hambur-Haque action (AU).}
```

::: notes
The money slide. Ninety seconds. Walk through the three traces: all
three central banks share the direction of policy around major global
events but differ sharply in timing and magnitude. US shock variance
compresses through the 2009-2015 ZLB period. UK target factor is
nonzero throughout. AU moves later and shallower. Pause before moving
on.
:::



## What mpshock does not yet do

- **Three countries only**: Euro-area (Jarocinski-Karadi ECB, Altavilla EA-MPD) is the v0.2.0 priority
- **Monthly aggregation sums event surprises**: Bu, Rogers, and Wu (2021) argue for `NA`-coding in LP-IV estimation
- **No new identification**: mpshock is a data-curation project, not a new econometric method
- **Licences vary**: users cite the underlying paper for any series used in academic work

\vspace{0.5em}

**v0.2.0 roadmap**: Euro-area shocks (Altavilla EA-MPD, JK ECB), Romer-Romer US narrative series, Bolhuis-Das-Yao cross-country panel subject to licence.

::: notes
Honest limitations. Name each one. Frame v0.2.0 as a commitment: the
Euro-area additions will take the panel from 3 to 4 currency areas.
Bu-Rogers-Wu NA-aggregation is already documented in each help file.
45 seconds.
:::



## Contact, code, paper

::: columns
::: {.column width="62%"}

```{=latex}
{\large\bfseries Charles Coverdale\par}
\vspace{0.5em}
\texttt{charles.f.coverdale@gmail.com}\\
\texttt{github.com/charlescoverdale/mpshock}\\
\texttt{charlescoverdale.github.io/publications}
\vspace{0.9em}

\textbf{Acknowledgements.} I thank the authors of each bundled series for making their data publicly available: Nakamura, Steinsson, Bauer, Swanson, Gürkaynak, Sack, Jarocinski, Karadi, Miranda-Agrippino, Ricco, Wu, Xia, Braun, Saha, Cesa-Bianchi, Thwaites, Vicondoa, Cloyne, Hürtgen, Hambur, Haque, and Beckers.
```

:::
::: {.column width="35%"}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/qrcode_paper.png}

\vspace{0.3em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Scan for paper PDF}
\end{center}

:::
:::

::: notes
Close cleanly. Name, email, GitHub, publications. Point at the QR
code. Acknowledge every author. Open for questions.
:::
